%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# B.3-2 The Exponentially Varying Sinusoid

> **Section Objective:** Identify, read, and sketch exponentially varying sinusoids quickly and accurately — the exact skills tested on exams.

---

Consider the signal x(t) = 4e^{-2t}cos(6t - 60°). This is an **exponentially varying sinusoid**: a cosine whose amplitude shrinks over time because it is multiplied by a decaying exponential.

### FAST RECOGNITION RULE

> If you see **exponential × cosine**, think: **oscillation trapped inside shrinking envelopes.**

Four things get tested on exams:
1. Identifying the signal type
2. Reading the time constant from e^{-at}
3. Reading the period from ω₀
4. Converting a phase angle in degrees into a time shift

This section will show you the fastest sketch method — envelopes first, sinusoid second — and the one trap that trips up most students.

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="book_image" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
![This textbook example shows the standard exam workflow: sketch the exponential envelope, sketch the shifted cosine, then combine them so the sinusoid is constrained between the upper and lower exponential envelopes.](/pages/page-022.png)

$$x(t) = A e^{-at} \cos(\omega_0 t + \theta)$$
*Parameter-reading checklist: A sets the starting amplitude scale; a sets the exponential decay rate so the time constant is τ = 1/a; ω₀ sets the oscillation speed so the period is T₀ = 2π/ω₀; and θ shifts the cosine horizontally by a time delay of −θ/ω₀.*

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="generate_image" data-teaching-role="exam_pattern_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
*📊 x(t) = 4e^{-2t}cos(6t - 60°) plotted with its upper and lower exponential envelopes. Every positive peak of the waveform touches the orange dashed curve; every negative peak touches the blue dashed curve. Faint vertical guides mark t = 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, and 2.0 s.*
![Chart](/generated/chart-1777142655518-04uld.png)

## 1. Fast Parameter Reading from the Formula

Given x(t) = 4e^{-2t}cos(6t - 60°), extract the exam-useful facts in one pass:

| Parameter | Symbol | Value | Meaning |
|-----------|--------|-------|---------|
| Amplitude scale | A | 4 | Starting height at t = 0 |
| Decay constant | a | 2 | Controls how fast envelope shrinks |
| Time constant | τ = 1/a | **0.5 s** | Envelope drops to ~37% of initial value |
| Angular frequency | ω₀ | 6 rad/s | Controls oscillation speed |
| Period | T₀ = 2π/ω₀ | **≈ 1 s** | Time for one full oscillation |
| Phase delay | θ | 60° | Cosine is delayed by (60/360) × 1 s ≈ **1/6 s** |

### COMMON TRAP

> **Do not confuse time constant 0.5 s with period ≈ 1 s.** One controls how fast the envelope decays; the other controls how fast the waveform oscillates. They are completely independent.

Shortest-solution checklist: identify a and compute τ = 1/a, identify ω₀ and compute T₀ = 2π/ω₀, then convert the phase angle to a fraction of T₀.

## 2. Fast Sketch Method: Envelopes First

Follow these four steps in order — do not skip to the sinusoid first.

**Step 1 — Draw the upper envelope +4e^{-2t}.**
Use anchor values:

| t (s) | 4e^{-2t} (approx.) |
|--------|---------------------|
| 0 | 4.00 |
| 0.5 | 1.47 |
| 1.0 | 0.54 |
| 1.5 | 0.20 |
| 2.0 | 0.07 |

Connect these points with a smooth, always-decreasing curve.

**Step 2 — Mirror it.** Reflect the upper envelope through the horizontal axis to get the lower envelope −4e^{-2t}.

**Step 3 — Plan the cosine.** The cosine cos(6t − 60°) has period ≈ 1 s and is delayed by about 1/6 s, so its first positive peak appears near t ≈ 1/6 s.

**Step 4 — Fit the oscillation inside the envelopes.** Sketch the sinusoid so that every positive peak **touches** +4e^{-2t} and every negative peak **touches** −4e^{-2t}.

### KEY INSIGHT

> **The envelopes are constraints, not extra signals.** They define the maximum possible amplitude at each moment; the sinusoid must stay within them.

#### Note
The signal is **not monotone**. Even though the amplitude shrinks, the cosine keeps flipping sign, so the waveform continues to oscillate — just with smaller and smaller swings.

$$x(t) = 4e^{-2t}\cos(6t - 60^\circ), \qquad x(t) = \pm\, 4e^{-2t} \text{ at positive and negative peaks}$$
*At every positive peak the cosine factor equals +1, so the waveform reaches exactly +4e^{-2t}; at every negative peak the cosine factor equals −1, so the waveform reaches exactly −4e^{-2t} — meaning peak amplitudes are fully determined by the exponential envelopes.*

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- Read τ = 1/a and T₀ = 2π/ω₀ directly from the formula — these are the two key derived quantities.
- Sketch envelopes ±Ae^{-at} first, then fit the phase-shifted cosine inside them.
- Time constant and period are independent: never substitute one for the other.

*In the next section we will keep using waveform-reading shortcuts on more signal forms.*

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-quiz-plan" data-quiz-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
